summary: I was born in Casper, Wyoming, because my father was working there at the time. He was involved with an independent wholesale optical laboratory with a man named Jack Lamb. Casper was just a temporary place for us before we moved to Muskogee, Oklahoma, where my dad took a job with Tom Brown Optical.

My mother grew up in Oklahoma, in a small farming town called Drummond, near Enid. Later, my family moved to Muskogee, Oklahoma, where I attended school from elementary through high school. Oklahoma has always been home to me and my family, and it shaped much of my upbringing and education.

Drummond is the small farming town in mid-central Oklahoma where my mother grew up. Her parents ran a café there, and it was a humble but important part of her early life. It’s where her father, a World War I hero, lived before the family faced some hardships.

Enid, Oklahoma, is near where my mother grew up, just outside Drummond. It was part of the region that shaped her early years and family life. Though I didn't live there myself, it’s a place connected to my maternal roots.

My maternal grandfather was a hero in World War I and was awarded the French Croix de Guerre for his service. His military experience was a point of pride in our family history, even though he had a difficult childhood afterward due to alcohol issues. The war and his service influenced the family legacy.

The Croix de Guerre was awarded to my grandfather for his heroic service in World War I. It’s a French military honor that recognized his bravery during the war. This decoration is a significant part of my family’s military heritage.

My father started his career as an errand boy for American Optical, which was one of the first companies he worked for in the optical industry. Later, he worked for other optical companies, but American Optical was where he got his start. It was part of the foundation for the family business that he eventually founded.

Tom Brown Optical was the company my father worked for when our family moved to Muskogee, Oklahoma. He took a job there after leaving Casper, and that move brought us to Muskogee where I attended school. It was an important step in his career before founding Coburn Optical.

My father also worked for Bausch & Lomb at one point during his career in the optical industry. It was one of several companies he gained experience with before starting his own business. This experience helped him build the skills needed to run Coburn Optical successfully.

Coburn Optical was the company my father founded after gaining experience with several optical firms. It was a family business that became quite successful, with my father’s brother and three of us sons joining at various times. We grew the business significantly, eventually taking it public and later selling it.

entity: O. W. Coburn School of Law
summary: The O. W. Coburn School of Law was named in honor of my father and was dedicated in 1979. The school was established through the efforts of Charles Coffey, who was my father's lawyer and believed in teaching reconciliation. Unfortunately, the law school closed in 1985 and was merged with Pat Robertson’s school.

Charles Coffey was a brilliant lawyer in Tulsa who served as my father's lawyer. He was instrumental in the founding of the O. W. Coburn School of Law, advocating for a law school focused on reconciliation rather than alienation. His vision aligned with my father's values, which led to the naming of the school after my dad.

Pat Robertson’s school absorbed the O. W. Coburn School of Law after it closed in 1985. While my father’s law school didn’t last long, it was notable that it ended up being merged with Robertson’s institution. This was part of the later history of the school named after my father.

Anita Hill was one of the professors at the O. W. Coburn School of Law before it closed. She later became well-known for her role in the Clarence Thomas Supreme Court nomination hearings. Although I never met her personally, her connection to the school is an interesting part of its history.

Clarence Thomas was involved in a major national controversy during his Supreme Court nomination, which included Anita Hill’s testimony. This event is linked indirectly to the O. W. Coburn School of Law through Hill’s role as a professor there. It’s a significant political moment connected to people associated with the school.

Longfellow Elementary School in Muskogee was the first school I remember attending. After my family moved from Casper to Muskogee, I started my education there. It was the beginning of my schooling before continuing through junior high and high school in the same city.

Muskogee, Oklahoma, is where my family settled after leaving Casper, Wyoming. My father took a job with Tom Brown Optical there, and I attended school from elementary through high school in Muskogee. It’s where I grew up and spent my formative years.

I graduated from Oklahoma State University in 1970 with a degree in accounting. I chose accounting because I believed that if you can account for things, you can manage them, which was important since I planned to join the family business. OSU was a key part of my preparation for my career.

I met Carolyn Denton, who was Miss Oklahoma in 1967, way back in the first grade. She was my best friend for years before we started dating, and we eventually married during her junior year of college. Carolyn’s talent was singing, which she showcased as Miss Oklahoma, and she passed that gift to our daughter Sarah.

Carolyn Denton was my childhood friend and later became Miss Oklahoma in 1967. We met in first grade and stayed close for years before marrying during her junior year of college. She was a talented singer, and our daughter Sarah inherited her musical abilities from her side of the family.

Our daughter Sarah was a national grand finalist in the 2001 Metropolitan Opera National Council Auditions. Her voice was described as a silvery, resonant soprano, and I’m very proud of her accomplishments. She inherited her singing talent from her mother’s side, not from me.

The Vietnam War was a significant backdrop during my college years. I observed that the war was poorly handled by leadership, with a lack of truth about casualty rates and the ground situation. Although I was drafted, I did not serve in Vietnam because of my draft lottery number, but my brothers served in the military during that time.

The Fall of Saigon in 1975 marked the end of the Vietnam War, an event I remember as a significant turning point. It symbolized the conclusion of a difficult and unpopular conflict that deeply affected the country. I witnessed the unrest and protests leading up to that moment during my college years.

My father served briefly in the United States Navy during World War II but was discharged because of a back injury he had from childhood. Although he couldn’t continue in the Navy, his service was part of our family’s military tradition. My brothers also served in the military during later conflicts.

One of my brothers served in the United States military and was stationed in South Korea during the Vietnam War era. Although he didn’t go to Vietnam, his service in South Korea was part of the broader military commitments of our family. It was a significant experience for him during that time.

My older brother served in the United States Air Force during the Vietnam War period. While he didn’t serve in Vietnam directly, his military service was an important part of our family’s contribution to the armed forces. The Air Force was his branch during that time.

Lyndon B. Johnson was president for much of the Vietnam War, and I believe his administration failed to be truthful about the war’s progress and casualties. His leadership, along with others, contributed to the public’s distrust and the eventual downfall of the war effort. I saw firsthand how important truth is in governance.

Richard Nixon succeeded Johnson as president during the Vietnam War and continued the pattern of not being fully truthful about the war. His administration also faced the challenge of ending the conflict, which they ultimately did by withdrawing. The leadership failures under both presidents shaped my view on government integrity.

Robert McNamara, as Secretary of Defense, was part of the leadership that failed to provide truthful information about the Vietnam War. His role in the administration contributed to the misinformation and poor handling of the war effort. This lack of honesty had serious consequences for the country and the military.

The 1968 Democratic National Convention was a chaotic event marked by protests and civil unrest related to the Vietnam War. It was a time when many Americans expressed their opposition to the war, and it reflected the deep divisions in the country. I recall it as a significant moment of national turmoil during my college years.

Agent Orange was a herbicide used during the Vietnam War that caused health problems for many veterans. I believed that benefits should be based on scientific evidence of injury, but the VA expanded compensation too broadly, which I opposed. I also worked on oversight to address fraud related to Agent Orange claims, emphasizing the need for integrity.

The United States Department of Justice once inspected my manufacturing operation because I refused to fill out their discrimination forms, confident that I was not discriminating. They came to inspect and verify, and I had a diverse workforce including African Americans, women, and immigrants. It was an example of standing up for principles and fairness in business.

Italy became a major player in the optical industry, overtaking American companies in frame production. By the time I left the family business, most frame manufacturing was controlled by Italian and Japanese companies, with no American-owned firms left. This shift reflected broader changes in global manufacturing and government policy failures.

Japan, along with Italy, took over much of the optical frame manufacturing industry that was once American. By the late 1970s, no American-owned companies remained in that sector. This was part of a larger trend of globalization and the decline of American manufacturing in certain industries.
